industry.label: Enterprise Software & Platform Engineering
industry.summary: This is an internal enterprise AI-platform role — the agentic platform is a horizontal capability that other business units build on. Frame answers for large, often regulated enterprises: deep integration with CRM/ERP/DevOps/data ecosystems, enterprise governance, compliance and audit, multi-tenant scale, and FinOps accountability across teams.
focusAreas: Enterprise system integration (CRM, ERP, DevOps, data platforms)
FinOps / cost accountability across business units
AI-first SDLC adoption across the engineering org

addendum.industryAngle: In this enterprise-platform context the coordinator is a shared service many business units invoke — so the bounded step/token budget doubles as a per-tenant cost guardrail, and the typed hand-off contract is exactly what lets a central platform team safely support workflows other teams authored.

The JD explicitly names Temporal IO and enterprise integrations (CRM/ERP). Durable execution matters here because an agent run spans multiple systems of record — a re-sent side effect like a duplicate ERP posting or CRM update is a business-data-integrity incident, not just a cosmetic glitch.

For a regulated enterprise the code-level approval gate and audit trail aren't just safety — they're what satisfies internal audit, SOX-style change controls, and compliance sign-off. Frame HITL as an enterprise-governance control wired to RBAC and retained, tamper-evident audit logs.

In this enterprise setting index-level RBAC must map onto existing enterprise identity and entitlements (SSO groups, data-classification tiers), and the audit of which documents were retrieved is a hard compliance requirement — not a nice-to-have.

The JD calls out FinOps by name. At enterprise scale the answer must include per-business-unit chargeback/showback and budgets, so spend is attributable to the team that drove it rather than buried in a single platform bill.

As a shared platform, the eval suite becomes a quality contract — effectively an SLA — with every consuming business unit. The CI gate protects all downstream teams at once, and new eval cases often originate from production incidents across multiple tenants.

For an enterprise platform team the abstraction layer is doubly important: consuming teams standardise on your interfaces, and you must support multi-LLM-provider routing (the JD calls this out) plus enterprise procurement/security constraints on which models and frameworks are actually approved for use.

The JD's 'drive AI-first SDLC across the organization' is the industry angle here — frame adoption as an enterprise change-management program spanning many teams, with a platform-team operating model and early security/compliance partnership, not a single-team win.
